title: Show Containers in a Clean Triage Table
hook: Turn noisy docker ps output into the few fields operators scan first.
problem: You need a fast view of container state, image, uptime, and published ports without wrapped columns hiding the important parts.
starting_state: Docker is installed and the host has one or more containers.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Docker prints container metadata in a narrower table.
when_to_use: Use during first-response container triage to see what is running, stopped, restarting, or exposing ports.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat it as an application health check; it reports Docker state, not whether the app works.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A table with container names, statuses, images, and port mappings.

shorts.title: Readable docker ps.
shorts.voiceover: When Docker output wraps into a mess, format it. Names, status, image, and ports are the first fields you need.
shorts.caption: Make docker ps readable during triage.

linkedin.question: What fields do you always want in your first Docker triage view?
linkedin.cta: Save this for the next time docker ps output is too noisy to scan.

youtube.description: List containers in an operator-friendly table without changing anything.

experiments.variant_a: Turn noisy docker ps output into a clean table.
experiments.variant_b: Your first Docker triage command should be readable.

title: Find Restarting Containers Fast
hook: Restart loops hide in plain sight unless you filter for them.
problem: A service is unstable and you need to quickly identify containers stuck restarting.
starting_state: Docker is running and at least one container may be in a restart loop.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Docker filters container metadata to restarting containers.
when_to_use: Use when an app is unavailable, flapping, or repeatedly failing startup checks.
when_not_to_use: Do not stop at this command; follow with recent logs for the failing container.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A table of restarting containers, or no rows if none match.

shorts.title: Find Docker restart loops.
shorts.voiceover: If a host feels broken, look for restart loops first. This filter shows only containers in restarting state.
shorts.caption: Filter Docker directly for restart loops.

linkedin.question: Do you check restart loops before digging into app logs?
linkedin.cta: Keep this one-liner handy for outage triage.

youtube.description: Quickly find Docker containers stuck in restarting state.

experiments.variant_a: Restart loops hide in plain sight.
experiments.variant_b: Before reading logs, find what is restarting.

title: Check Container Health Status
hook: Docker may say a container is running while its health check says otherwise.
problem: You need to see health-check state without opening a full inspect dump.
starting_state: A target container exists and may have a Docker health check configured.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Docker reads container state metadata.
when_to_use: Use when a container is running but the service still seems unavailable.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a full app check if the image has no HEALTHCHECK.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.

shorts.title: Running is not healthy.
shorts.voiceover: Running only means the process exists. If the image has a health check, print both Docker status and health status.
shorts.caption: Check Docker health without the full inspect dump.

linkedin.question: How often do you see running containers that are still unhealthy?
linkedin.cta: Add this to your Docker incident checklist.

youtube.description: Show container health and runtime status in one formatted inspect line.

experiments.variant_a: Running is not the same as healthy.
experiments.variant_b: Docker status can lie by omission.

title: Read Recent Container Logs
hook: Skip the million-line log scroll and read only the recent failure window.
problem: A container is failing and full logs are too large or too noisy.
starting_state: A target container exists and has recent logs.
what_changed: Nothing changes locally. Docker reads recent log output for one container.
when_to_use: Use when checking recent crashes, startup errors, HTTP failures, or deployment regressions.
when_not_to_use: Do not paste raw logs into public channels without reviewing them for secrets or user data.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: The last 100 log lines from the previous 10 minutes.

shorts.title: Docker logs without the flood.
shorts.voiceover: For triage, do not start with every log line ever written. Use since and tail together.
shorts.caption: Read the failure window, not the whole log history.

linkedin.question: What time window do you usually start with when reading container logs?
linkedin.cta: Use this pattern before opening full logs.

youtube.description: Read recent Docker logs without flooding the terminal.

experiments.variant_b: Start with the last 10 minutes of logs.

title: Snapshot Container CPU and Memory
hook: Get Docker resource usage once, without leaving a live dashboard running.
problem: You need a quick CPU and memory snapshot for running containers.
starting_state: Docker is running with one or more active containers.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Docker reads current resource counters.
when_to_use: Use when a host is slow, memory pressure is suspected, or one container may be noisy.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat one snapshot as a trend; repeat it or use monitoring for longer windows.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A table showing container CPU percentage, memory usage, network I/O, and block I/O.

shorts.title: Docker stats once.
shorts.voiceover: Docker stats defaults to a live stream. Add no-stream for a clean one-time snapshot.
shorts.caption: Use --no-stream for a triage snapshot.

linkedin.question: Do you prefer one-shot resource snapshots or live terminal dashboards during incidents?
linkedin.cta: Save this for quick host pressure checks.

youtube.description: Capture current Docker resource usage without changing the system.

experiments.variant_b: Stop leaving docker stats running during triage.

title: Show Published Container Ports
hook: When a service is unreachable, confirm Docker is publishing the port you think it is.
problem: You need to map container ports to host ports without scanning firewall rules.
starting_state: A target container exists and may publish one or more ports.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Docker reads port mapping metadata.
when_to_use: Use when HTTP, SSH, database, or admin ports are unreachable from outside the container.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it alone for firewall diagnosis; a published port can still be blocked upstream.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.

shorts.title: Find Docker port mappings.
shorts.voiceover: If the app is unreachable, first confirm Docker published the port. docker port shows the mapping directly.
shorts.caption: Check the actual Docker port map.

linkedin.question: What is your first check when a containerized web service is unreachable?
linkedin.cta: Keep this command in your web service triage notes.

youtube.description: Quickly inspect published Docker ports for a specific container.

experiments.variant_a: Confirm Docker published the port you think it did.
experiments.variant_b: Unreachable service? Check the Docker port map first.

title: Summarize Docker Disk Usage
hook: See how Docker storage is split across images, containers, volumes, and cache.
problem: The host disk is full and you need a safe storage summary before cleanup.
starting_state: Docker is installed and using local storage.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Docker reads storage accounting data.
when_to_use: Use before cleanup decisions to understand whether images, containers, volumes, or build cache are responsible.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat reclaimable space as safe to delete until you verify ownership and backups.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A Docker disk usage summary with reclaimable space estimates.

shorts.title: Docker disk usage safely.
shorts.voiceover: When Docker fills a disk, do not start with prune. Start with docker system df -v.
shorts.caption: Inspect Docker disk usage before deleting anything.

linkedin.question: What Docker storage category has surprised you most during disk incidents?
linkedin.cta: Run this before reaching for cleanup commands.

youtube.description: Inspect Docker disk usage safely before cleanup.

experiments.variant_a: Do not start Docker disk triage with prune.
experiments.variant_b: Find the Docker disk culprit before deleting anything.

title: Inspect Container Environment Names
hook: Check what environment variables exist without printing their secret values.
problem: You need to know whether expected env vars are present, but dumping values may expose credentials.
starting_state: A target container exists and has environment variables configured.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Docker reads container configuration and the shell redacts values in terminal output.
when_to_use: Use when checking whether config keys are present during auth, database, or API failures.
when_not_to_use: Do not rely on this for perfect secret handling in shell history, logs, or copied output from other commands.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.

shorts.title: Check env vars without leaking values.
shorts.voiceover: Docker inspect can print secrets if you are careless. This version lists environment variable names and redacts values.
shorts.caption: Inspect config keys without dumping secrets.

linkedin.question: Have you seen secrets accidentally pasted from docker inspect output?
linkedin.cta: Use redacted config checks when sharing triage output.

youtube.description: Inspect Docker environment variable names while redacting values from terminal output.

experiments.variant_b: Check env vars without printing credentials.

title: See Container Network Attachments
hook: A container can be healthy and still attached to the wrong network.
problem: Services cannot reach each other and you need to confirm container network membership.
starting_state: A target container exists and is attached to at least one Docker network.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Docker reads container networking metadata.
when_to_use: Use when containers cannot connect by service name, expected networks are missing, or IP assumptions look wrong.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a packet-level diagnosis; follow up with DNS and connectivity checks when needed.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.

shorts.title: Check Docker networks.
shorts.voiceover: If containers cannot talk, check network membership before changing config.
shorts.caption: Healthy container, wrong network? Check this.

linkedin.question: How often are container networking bugs really attachment or naming issues?
linkedin.cta: Add this to your container connectivity checklist.

youtube.description: Inspect which Docker networks a container is attached to and what IP address it has.

experiments.variant_a: A healthy container can still be on the wrong network.
experiments.variant_b: Container connectivity starts with network membership.

title: Review Recent Docker Events
hook: Docker keeps a recent event trail for starts, stops, pulls, and health changes.
problem: Something changed on the host and you need a quick timeline of recent Docker activity.
starting_state: Docker daemon is running and has recent event history available.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Docker reads daemon events for a bounded time window.
when_to_use: Use after an unexplained restart, image pull, container stop, or health-status change.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat it as a permanent audit log; Docker events are not a compliance log.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Timestamped Docker events such as start, die, pull, health_status, or restart.

shorts.title: Docker's recent activity trail.
shorts.voiceover: When something changed and nobody knows what, check recent Docker events for starts, stops, pulls, and restarts.
shorts.caption: Use Docker events for a quick change timeline.

linkedin.question: Do you check Docker events during incident timelines?
linkedin.cta: Try this before digging through every service log.

youtube.description: Use docker events with a time window to inspect recent Docker daemon activity safely.

experiments.variant_a: Docker has a recent activity trail.
experiments.variant_b: Need an incident timeline? Start with Docker events.
